course_title: Physiology of Systems & Clinical Application to Speech Pathology Practice
course_description:
Physiology of Systems & Clinical Application to Speech Pathology Practice

PHYL7068 is designed forﾠspeech pathology students to gain an in-depth knowledge of human physiology in direct correlation with communication and swallowing functions.

By the end of the course, students will:

The course delves into the general and specialised aspects of cell and system physiology, focusing on the neurological underpinnings of speech and language and the physiology of the cardiorespiratory, muscle, gastrointestinal, metabolic and renal systems, frequently relating these to communication and swallowing.

This course is delivered on UQExtend , an online learning platform designed to be interactive and engaging. It allows you to work through the content at your own pace, no matter where you are or how many classes or other responsibilities you balance in your schedule.

Each module is supported by weekly masterclasses —interactive workshops that help you deepen your understanding of the content.

Additionally, you will participate in practical classes throughout the semester, further reinforcing your understanding of the course material.

These face-to-face sessions will also include some assessments.

companions: You'll need to complete the following courses at the same time:
restrictions: Available for Speech Pathology students only

course_contacts.name: Student and Academic Administration Team
course_contacts.notes: The SBMS Student and Academic Administration Team is located on Level 1 of the Sir William Macgregor Building (64-130).

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: To enable speech pathology students to develop an understanding of physiological systems and mechanisms as they relate to communication and swallowing, and an appreciation of relationship between these underlying mechanisms, clinical presentations and treatment options.

learning_outcomes.description: Describe general and specialised physiology of the cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, sensory and motor nervous systems in healthy and aging populations and discuss how they relate to communication and swallowing.
Analyse physiological basis of diseases in individuals with communication and swallowing disorders.
Apply knowledge of pathophysiology to identify the clinical presentation of individuals with communication and swallowing disorders and evaluate pharmacological treatment options.
Apply techniques used in physiological experimentation, including hypothesis testing, methods of data collection and analysis, to evaluate experimental results.
Demonstrate collaborative learning skills and effective communication in a group setting.
Reflect and synthesise effectively on your learning in physiology.

assessment_summary.title: Assessment 1A: Case Study Questions
assessment_summary.due_date: 24/03/2025 - 28/03/2025; The quiz will open at 12:00 pm on the Monday of Week 5 and close at 12:00 pm on the Friday of Week 5.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1A: Case Study Questions
assessment_details.due_date: 24/03/2025 - 28/03/2025 The quiz will open at 12:00 pm on the Monday of Week 5 and close at 12:00 pm on the Friday of Week 5.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: For this assessment, the process will begin at the start of Week 2, when you will receive a detailed case study, along with a set of questions designed to deepen your understanding of biochemical processes (covered in Module 1) and the nervous system (covered in Module 2). These materials are intended to help you explore how these topics connect to specific health and disease scenarios. You will have the duration of these modules to formulate your responses. The assessment itself will take place online via a 30-minute quiz on Blackboard, available in week 5. During the quiz, you will be presented with 3 to 4 of the previously provided questions to answer and submit. Please note that you will only have one opportunity to access and complete this quiz. Preparation is key; if you are well-prepared, you will likely perform excellently on this assessment. We recommend attending your workshops, where you can work with your peers and lecturers to ensure your answers are correct. More details are provided in the 'Assessment' folder on Blackboard. It is very important to note that all answers must be your own individual work. You must make a valid attempt at all questions for your assessment to be considered a 'reasonable attempt’, which is necessary to pass this assessment. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . To pass this assessment, students must demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submissions independent of AI tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You will complete this assessment through Blackboard within the date range.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Requests for extension must be made prior to the submission deadline, even if you are awaiting your supporting documentation. Late requests for extension will not be accepted for consideration unless you provide evidence of exceptional circumstances preventing you from submitting a request prior to the deadline, e.g., due to hospitalisation.
If you have been granted an extension, then the assessment specified penalty listed under Late Submission will be applied to submissions made after the due date of the approved extension.
Late submission
No late submissions possible.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1B - Case Study Concept Map
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: For this assessment, you will receive a case study that outlines a client's health situation. Your main task is to analyse the details of the case and synthesise this information into a mind map. The mind map should: Clearly depict the client’s health conditions. Demonstrate how different biological systems are interconnected in the client’s case. Objectives: This assessment is designed to enhance your understanding of the interactions between biological systems in health and disease contexts. Mind maps will help retain complex details, foster critical thinking, and enhance collaborative learning. Group Activity: You will complete this task within a group setting. Groups will be formed during your Masterclasses. Peer Evaluation As part of this assessment, a peer evaluation will account for 5% of your final mark. This evaluation will be conducted using Buddy Check on Blackboard, where you will assess your team members based on their ability to collaborate effectively throughout the assessment. Completion Requirement: You must complete the peer evaluation to earn the 5% allocated to this component. Failure to complete the peer evaluation will result in a score of 0% for this part of the assessment, regardless of your participation. Additional Support and Information: Detailed information about this assessment will be provided during your Masterclasses. Throughout the semester, sessions will be available to discuss any questions or concerns regarding this assessment with the course coordinator. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . To pass this assessment, students must demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submissions independent of AI tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit your Assessment task, with the electronic coversheet available on the Schools website ( under Undergraduate - Assessment ), to Turnitin by the submission deadline.
You may submit drafts to Turnitin up to the due date, but you will only be able to submit once on or after the due date.
You should also keep an electronic copy of every piece of assessment you submit.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Requests for extension must be made prior to the submission deadline, even if you are awaiting your supporting documentation. Late requests for extension will not be accepted for consideration unless you provide evidence of exceptional circumstances preventing you from submitting a request prior to the deadline, e.g., due to hospitalisation.
If you have been granted an extension, then the assessment specified late submission penalty in the table above will apply to submissions made after the due date of the approved extension.
Please note - Irrespective of whether an extension has been granted for one or more students in the group, your group must submit the Case Study by the due date. This submission will be evaluated by the Course Coordinators to determine that sufficient progress has been made however, there will be no marks or feedback provided for this submission. If we feel you're not progressing, we will arrange a meeting to discuss this.
An extension on Case Study Part B does not automatically qualify your group for additional time to complete Part C.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
If you are experiencing technical difficulties uploading your submission, please email a copy of your assessment to sbms@enquire.uq.edu.au so this can be logged on your behalf before the due date.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1C - Case Study Video
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: For Part C of your assessment, you'll explore the concluding chapter of Casey's narrative. You will be required to integrate the knowledge acquired from Assessment Parts A and B with newly introduced biological systems. Collaborating within your group, you'll create and deliver a presentation responding to a specific prompt about Casey's experience. This presentation should be recorded and submitted for marking. This assessment encourages the synthesis of complex information across different stages of learning and the development of communication skills and teamwork as you articulate and share your insights on Casey's journey. Peer Evaluation As part of this assessment, a peer evaluation will account for 5% of your final mark. This evaluation will be conducted using Buddy Check on Blackboard, where you will assess your team members based on their ability to collaborate effectively throughout the assessment. Completion Requirement: You must complete the peer evaluation to earn the 5% allocated to this component. Failure to complete the peer evaluation will result in a score of 0% for this part of the assessment, regardless of your participation. More details can be seen in your 'Assessment' folder on Blackboard. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. assessment_details.title: End of Semester Exam
assessment_details.task_description: The end-of-semester exam in this course is designed to assess your comprehensive understanding of the covered material. Exam Format: Type: Closed-book, on-campus exam using Inspera. You will need to bring your own laptop with you to the exam Question Types: Multiple-choice, short-answer, label the images, fill in the blanks, and other assorted questions Content Coverage: The exam will cover all lectures, practical material, and modules, with the focus proportional to the number of lectures in each module. Attendance at modules, workshops, and practical classes is highly recommended, as there will be explicit questions on these sections in the exam. This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. More details of the examination format will be provided on Blackboard during the semester. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 120 minutes Calculator options (In person) Casio FX82 series only or UQ approved and labelled calculator Open/closed book Closed Book examination - no written materials permitted Exam platform Inspera Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The exam will be a closed book invigilated exam held on campus, undertaken via the Inspera eAssessment platform. Students will be required to bring a laptop to the exam that meets the device requirements for Inspera. It is important that you check before the exam that your laptop meets the device requirements for using the Inspera Assessment platform. If you do not own a suitable laptop, you can borrow one from the Library . The Library website Get familiar with Inspera provides the latest information for students about using Inspera. Further information about the exam will be provided on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam .

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Module 4: Respiration and the process of speech This online module focuses on the physiology of how we breathe, which is respiration. We will then relate this to articulation, resonation, and phonation in speech production. Associated Workshops: Week 11: Activities will cover Module 5

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (24 Feb - 01 Jun)
learning_activities.topic: Weekly Masterclasses Masterclasses are offered every fortnight to help you consolidate the information from each of your modules. To maximise the benefits of the masterclasses, it is highly recommended that you engage with and work through your online modules to the best of your ability before attending. This preparation will enable you to participate actively, understand advanced concepts more clearly, and gain deeper insights from these sessions. However, we recognise that keeping pace with the coursework can sometimes be challenging. If you find yourself falling behind, please don't be discouraged - attending the masterclasses will still be beneficial, as they will help you to catch up and clarify doubts. Remember, while preparation is key to enhancing your learning experience, our masterclasses are designed to be inclusive and supportive, ensuring everyone gains valuable knowledge, regardless of their starting point. Note: These classes are NOT lectures. Within these workshops, you will have activities to work through with your classmates to help you learn and/or consolidate your knowledge. You will also have an assessment in each (please see the assessment section). Your lecturers will facilitate these classes, allowing you some 1:1 time to discuss anything you are confused about.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 2 (24 Feb - 09 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Module 1: Biological foundations & homeostasis Module 1 of your UQExtend Online Learning aims to establish a strong foundation for understanding key concepts in speech pathology. It starts with defining physiology and its importance in this field, then explores chemical basics essential for biological functions. The focus then shifts to homeostasis, vital for bodily balance, encompassing negative and positive feedback mechanisms. We'll also examine cellular components, appreciating the complexity of organelles and how cells form tissues and organs. Concluding with an overview of major body systems, this module sets the stage for deeper, specialised discussions in subsequent modules, ensuring a comprehensive and integrated understanding for all students. Associated workshops: Week 1: Introduction to the course Week 3: Module 1 Content

Physiology Practical 1 - Osmosis The "Osmosis" practical demonstrates the mechanisms by which the fluids move within the body's compartments. This has important implications for how the body maintains fluid balance and the effect of introducing solutions (such as by intravenous injection) to the body. This is completed on campus in the laboratory. Students are to remember to bring their PPE, lab coat, and closed-in shoes.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 4 (10 Mar - 23 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Module 2: The Nervous and Muscular System Module 2 of your UQExtend Online Learning, focuses on the brain, spinal cord, and muscle physiology. Understanding these areas is crucial for treating conditions like stuttering or post-stroke communication issues stemming from nervous system dysfunctions. The module covers basics to system-level understanding, including control of muscles and sensory feedback, and highlights neuro- and muscle physiology important for speech pathology. It delves into motor and sensory systems and movement disorders. Associated Workshops: Week 5: Activities will cover Module 2

Physiology Practical 2: Action Potential [Online] The "Action Potential" practical features a computer-based module designed to showcase the electrical properties of nerves. This activity can be completed at the student's own pace but should be finished by the end of this week.

Physiology Practical 3 - Skeletal Muscle The "Skeletal Muscle" practical further examines muscle contraction, investigating how the strength of muscle contractions is controlled. This is completed on campus in the laboratory. Students are to remember to bring their PPE, lab coat, and closed-in shoes.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 5 To Week 6 (24 Mar - 06 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Module 3: The Cardiovascular system This online learning module explores the cardiovascular system. It begins by examining how oxygenated blood is pumped by the heart throughout the body and how deoxygenated blood carrying carbon dioxide returns to the lungs for gas exchange. Emphasizing their interdependent relationship, the module covers the mechanics of cardiac conduction and contraction and the process of air intake, oxygen transport, and carbon dioxide expulsion. While these systems are initially taught separately, their overlapping functions are highlighted. Associated Workshops: Week 7: Activities will cover Module 3

Physiology Practical 6 - Blood pressure The "Blood Pressure" practical examines the measurement of arterial blood pressure and the effect of posture and stress on Blood Pressure. You will also investigate the venous circulation and listen to the heart sounds. This is completed on campus in the laboratory. Students are to remember to bring their PPE, lab coat, and closed-in shoes.

Physiology Practical 4: Respiration [Online] "Respiration" is an online clinical scenario based on a respiratory case. This activity can be completed at the student's own pace but should be finished by the end of this week.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 10 (28 Apr - 11 May)
learning_activities.topic: Module 5: The Gastrointestinal system In this online module, you will learn about the gastrointestinal system, including the essential processes of ingestion, swallowing, motility, and mechanical and chemical digestion. Associated Workshops: Week 13: Activities will cover Module 6

Physiology Practical 5 - Spirometry "Spirometry" is a practical session focused on measuring lung volumes and airflow, along with exploring the factors affecting these measurements. This is completed on campus in the laboratory. Students are to remember to bring their PPE, lab coat, and closed-in shoes.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 11 To Week 12 (12 May - 25 May)
learning_activities.topic: Module 6: Sensory Neurobiology, Mastication and Deglutition In sensory neurobiology students will explore sensory reception, with a focus on sensory systems that are highly relevant to speech pathology – the auditory, gustatory, and olfactory systems. Whilst in the second part of this module students will focus on mastication and swallowing in depth. The goal of this final module is for students to integrate previous knowledge in neuroanatomy and physiology to obtain an advanced understanding of body processes. Associated Workshops: Week 12: Activities will cover Module 6.1 Week 13: Quiz or Module 5 & 6.1. Activities will cover Module 6.2
